critique.summary: The article describes Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that offers an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It highlights features such as pan and zoom capabilities, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git status panels, smart detection of URLs and file paths, live settings editing, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon provides an infinite 2D canvas to organize terminal sessions as movable and resizable panels.
It supports multiple color-coded workspaces with various layout modes and a minimap for navigation.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color, mouse reporting, and other advanced features.
Integration with AI agents like Claude Code and Codex allows persistent sessions and token usage tracking.
Built-in git integration shows repository status and diffs without context switching.
Smart detection enables clickable URLs and file paths directly from the terminal output.
Settings can be edited live with instant application of changes, and sessions persist across restarts.
The project is open source, written in Rust, and uses modern GPU rendering technologies.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Horizon's features are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which is a credible source for this software project. The description of its capabilities, such as infinite canvas, AI agent integration, and git status panels, matches the documented features. The use of the Alacritty terminal engine and Rust for development is verifiable from the repository. There are no apparent factual errors or misleading statements in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention any potential limitations or system requirements beyond a brief note about needing Rust 1.85+ and Linux GPU headers.
There is no discussion of performance benchmarks or comparisons with other terminal emulators.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and handling terminal sessions are not addressed.
User experience aspects such as learning curve or accessibility features are not covered.
The article does not provide external reviews or user feedback to validate usability claims.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal, with users sharing their experiences, feature requests, and comparisons to other tools. Some highlight usability improvements, others express enthusiasm for the novel approach, while a few suggest simpler or alternative solutions.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: Provides detailed, experience-based feedback with constructive suggestions and points out specific technical issues, indicating a knowledgeable and accurate perspective.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: Offers practical UI/UX improvement ideas grounded in user experience, enhancing the tool's usability for power users.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and user perspective that helps frame the tool's niche and potential user base.

comments.author: iknowstuff
comments.text:
Hmm I guess I’d like my terminal to remember my shell sessions like that and just lef me open them in multiple windows so I can use my window manager to arrange as I please.

And then I’d like my window manager to be infinitely scrollable like a canvas with like 3 fingers on my touchpad. Do any exist like that?
comments.analysis: Suggests integration with existing window management paradigms, reflecting a user-centered approach to workflow enhancement.

comments.author: cedws
comments.text:
Spatial memory is really underutilised in computing.

When libghostty[0] releases maybe you could use that so you don't have to build everything from scratch.

[0]: https://mitchellh.com/writing/libghostty-is-coming
comments.analysis: Offers a practical technical recommendation that could improve development efficiency and leverage existing solutions.

comments.author: jauntywundrkind
comments.text:
Feels very Niri like, in a positive way. Niri's "scrollable" interface always has room to the right & down!

One of the Niri tools I built for myself is to let me insert a new row up top, to expand up. Thats afaik not super easy in base Niri, constraints the spatial growth some.

I'm pretty close to having some persistence of terminal windows, tmux, etc in Niri, with some tools to dump current state nicely. Doing an actual restore operation would be a good thing to build towards.
comments.analysis: Provides relevant comparisons and suggests valuable features, showing insight into terminal interface design.

comments.author: phrmendes
comments.analysis: Offers a dismissive comment without engaging with the unique aspects of Horizon, lacking constructive input.

comments.author: hokkos
comments.text: The last thing I want is the figma experience for my terminal
comments.analysis: Expresses a subjective dislike for complex GUI-like terminal interfaces, reflecting a common sentiment but without detailed reasoning.

comments.author: tamimio
comments.text:
Amazing, I will give it a try!

I played with egui before, I really enjoyed it, but I remember I stumbled across some issues had to use dioxus instead.
comments.analysis: Shares personal experience with related GUI frameworks, indicating a willingness to explore Horizon despite past challenges.

comments.author: riquito
comments.text: I love these kind of projects trying out non mainstream approaches! Thanks for sharing
comments.analysis: Positively reinforces innovation and encourages the developer, though the comment is brief and lacks technical depth.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: Expresses a creative but somewhat unrelated expectation, showing enthusiasm but not directly addressing the project.

comments.author: sakopov
comments.text: Will there be any built releases available?
comments.analysis: A straightforward inquiry about availability, useful for potential users but not analytical.
